channel.title: Ken Reid's Blog
channel.description: Data science, photography, books, and everything in between.

item.title: What We Leave Behind
item.description: From the plutonium in the planet's crust to the Golden Record in interstellar space to the memory of a Tuesday coffee, a look at everything we leave behind, and why none of us ever truly vanishes.

item.title: Optimizing Your Schedule (or: Treating Your Week Like a Solvable Problem)
item.description: What an operations researcher actually means by optimizing a schedule: objectives, constraints and weightings, a values clarification grid, and a working integer-programming model of your week. Nerd mode optional.

item.title: What Was I Made For?
item.description: On finite time, micro-ambition, Gandalf, Tim Minchin, and why every 'one day' dream needs a plan or it disappears.

item.title: M*A*S*H in the Modern Era: Comedy, Trauma, and the 4077th
item.description: A modern look at M*A*S*H: how a Korean War sitcom from the 1970s remains one of the sharpest critiques of bureaucracy, war, and trauma in television history.

item.title: Iain M. Banks and The Culture
item.description: Smashing the world requires only a hammer; building a better one requires an imagination. A reflection on Iain M. Banks's Culture series and the fierce contrarian act of imagining a future that works.

item.title: The Individualization of Responsibility
item.description: Corporations reframe systemic crises as consumer morality plays. From plastic bags to five-minute showers, how the public ends up feeling guilty while industrial actors avoid regulation.

item.title: Advice to My Younger Self
item.description: Forty things I would tell my younger self, from pensions and emergency funds to sunscreen, boundaries, and uncomfortable conversations.

item.title: Why Nobody Thanks the Person Who Stopped the Disaster
item.description: We celebrate the people who fix disasters and take preventive work for granted. That's a bug in how we think, and it costs us.

item.title: The Books I Recommend to Friends
item.description: The thirteen books I actually push on friends, grouped by the kind of friend I'd press them into the hands of.

item.title: When I Ended Up in Italy Because of Mistaken Identity
item.description: How a mix-up over an email address sent me to the northern Italian mountains to photograph a tech event I had no business being at.

item.title: How to Write a Blog (or: What I Learned By Doing It)
item.description: What I learned about writing a blog: why the word itself put me off, how I found a process, and what to do when people hate your work.

item.title: The Hidden Cost of Cobalt
item.description: The batteries in our phones, laptops, and EVs are largely made of cobalt mined in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, often by hand and often by children. A reflection on a presentation I gave, and what we can actually do about it.

item.title: ISO 8601 and the Date Cult I Happily Joined
item.description: A playful, half-joking ode to ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) as the only sortable, unambiguous, international date format worth using.

item.title: Accessibility-First Product Design in Data Science
item.description: Most data science outputs are inaccessible by default. Treating accessibility as the design constraint, not the audit step, produces clearer plots, better dashboards, and more rigorous communication for everyone.

item.title: The Life and Death of the Early Internet
item.description: From CRT monitors and Comet Cursor to MSN Messenger, Kazaa, Warcraft 3 forums, and Bebo: a personal history of how the early internet shaped who we are.

item.title: From Scotland to Michigan
item.description: From Scotland to Michigan: a personal account of culture shock, free education, healthcare, xenophobia, weather, and what it means to belong somewhere new.

item.title: The Ethics of LLM Use (Not LLMs)
item.description: An AI researcher's honest take on LLM ethics: the energy numbers, the cobalt hypocrisy, the Jevons paradox, and why telling people not to use AI on environmental grounds is a privilege position.

item.title: Dungeon Crawler Carl and the Strange Dignity of LitRPG
item.description: A book snob's confession: how Dungeon Crawler Carl used absurd LitRPG mechanics to deliver one of the most devastating emotional payloads in modern fantasy.

item.title: Fifteen Years of Silence: Patrick Rothfuss, The Doors of Stone, and the Architecture of Creative Friction
item.description: Fifteen years since The Wise Man's Fear and still no Doors of Stone. A personal reflection on the Kingkiller Chronicle, the long wait, the charity debacle, and why writing at this level is genuinely hard.

item.title: No Idea? No Problem: A Beginner's Guide to Building Your Data Science Portfolio
item.description: A practical guide to building a data science portfolio when you have no idea where to start. Covers project types, documentation, novelty, collaboration, and working in the GenAI era.

item.title: Evolutionary Computation's Identity Crisis in the Age of GenAI
item.description: Evolutionary Computation is having an identity crisis in the GenAI era, despite delivering major real-world wins in engineering, logistics, and constrained optimization.

item.title: In Defense of Audiobooks
item.description: The stigma against audiobooks is rooted in bad assumptions about learning, intelligence, and what counts as reading. The research doesn't support any of it.

item.title: Why It Still Matters to Learn to Code in the Age of AI
item.description: Coding still matters because the value is not syntax memorization. It is learning to break down problems, reason across systems, and build reliable solutions.

item.title: Your Professional Second Brain for Local LLM Work
item.description: A practical system for project documentation, knowledge management, and local LLM workflows that improves delivery, reviews, and project re-entry.

item.title: Snowball vs Avalanche: The Science of Paying Off Debt
item.description: Snowball and avalanche both work, but for different reasons. A practical guide with simulator-backed charts on interest, timing, and motivation.

item.title: Book Ratings Are Broken
item.description: 569 books rated, and the star system fails at all of them.

item.title: Why Most Self-Help Books Are Trash (And Which Ones Aren't)
item.description: The bad ones are blog posts stretched to 300 pages. The good ones cite their sources.

item.title: Building a Photo Tagging System with CLIP
item.description: How I used OpenCLIP to automatically classify 370 photos into 10 categories in under a minute — zero API cost, running entirely on CPU.
